topic: An employee is asked to train their replacement
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed, a grating soundtrack to my rising irritation. Sarah, the new hire, sat across the desk, radiating an unnerving level of calm. "So, I'll be taking over the Q3 reporting," she stated, her voice smooth as polished glass. I gripped the edge of my desk, my knuckles turning white. Every word she spoke felt like a deliberate dismantling of my established workflow. "You're going to walk me through every single legacy system, step-by-step," I managed, my tone sharper than intended. A tightness coiled in my chest, making it hard to draw a full breath. I watched her nod, that serene expression never wavering, and I felt a hot flush creep up my neck. I wanted to shout, to tell her that I’d done this work a thousand times, but the words caught in my throat, replaced by a prickly, restless energy that made my hands twitch.

[story 2]

"I need you to mentor Alex," Mr. Henderson announced, clapping his hands together with an unsettling cheerfulness. The air in the conference room immediately thickened. I stared at the man, my jaw clenching so hard I could feel the strain in my molars. Mentoring? Me? The thought ignited a furious heat behind my eyes. I shifted in my seat, the cheap plastic chair squeaking in protest. "Sir," I began, forcing my voice to remain level, though the tremor beneath it betrayed me, "with all due respect, my current workload is already at capacity. I don't have the bandwidth for a full handover right now." I leaned forward, my gaze fixed on the knot of his tie, refusing to meet his eyes. The silence stretched, heavy and suffocating, punctuated only by the frantic drumming of my own pulse against my ribs.

[story 3]

I paced the length of my cubicle, the worn carpet muffling the sound of my agitated footsteps. When Mark brought up the topic of training the incoming analyst, I stopped dead in my tracks, planting my hands firmly on my hips. "Are you serious?" I asked, the disbelief coloring my voice, making it sound brittle. My breathing became shallow and quick. I felt a profound sense of injustice wash over me; it was as if someone had just stolen the last piece of control I possessed. "I’ve been doing this role for five years. I know the intricacies. Why can't someone else handle this?" I watched the analyst nervously adjust their notepad, their posture radiating a quiet confidence that only amplified my internal turmoil. I wanted to stride over there and simply dismantle the entire premise of the request.
